9:0.rendering: While Ezra was praying and confessing, weeping and throwing himself down before the house of God, a very large assembly of Israelites, men, women, and children, gathered to him from Israel, and the people wept bitterly.
9:0.note: Ezra's public collapse in prayer pulls an entire community into shared tears, echoing the unified repentance at the temple dedication in 1 Kings 8.

9:1.rendering: Shecaniah son of Jehiel, one of the sons of Elam, spoke up and said to Ezra, "We have been unfaithful to our God and have married foreign women from the peoples of the land. But even now there is hope for Israel in spite of this."
9:1.note: Shecaniah's bold confession turns corporate guilt into the first crack of hope, echoing the ancient pattern that acknowledging covenant breach is the doorway to restoration.

9:2.rendering: Now then, let us make a covenant with our God to send away all these wives and their children, according to the counsel of my lord and of those who tremble at the commandment of our God. Let it be done according to the law.
9:2.note: The Hebrew verb for "send away" echoes God's earlier command to Abraham to "go out" from his father's house, creating a painful inversion of the original exodus call.

9:3.rendering: Arise, for the matter is on you, but we will be with you. Be strong and act.
9:3.note: This terse command to Ezra echoes the exact phrasing used to commission Joshua for conquest in Deuteronomy 31:6-7 and 1 Chronicles 28:20, transforming a call to military courage into one of communal repentance.

9:4.rendering: Then Ezra arose and made the leading priests, the Levites, and all Israel swear that they would do according to this word. So they swore.
9:4.note: Ezra's bold command echoes Moses at Sinai, rallying leaders to bind themselves by oath in a fresh covenant moment.

9:5.rendering: Then Ezra rose from before the house of God and went into the chamber of Jehohanan the son of Eliashib. He went there, but he ate no bread and drank no water, for he was mourning over the faithlessness of the exiles.
9:5.note: Ezra's fasting echoes Moses on Sinai and Elijah in the wilderness, turning personal grief into intercessory solidarity with a broken community.

9:6.rendering: A proclamation was issued throughout Judah and Jerusalem that all the returned exiles should assemble in Jerusalem.
9:6.note: This public summons echoes the ancient covenant renewal assemblies, gathering the community to confront their shared unfaithfulness after the exile.

9:7.rendering: Whoever does not come within three days, according to the decision of the leaders and the elders, all his property will be devoted to God and he himself will be separated from the assembly of the exiles.
9:7.note: The Hebrew word for "devoted" is herem, the same term for total destruction of Canaanite idols, now turned inward on the community's own compromised possessions.

9:8.rendering: All the men of Judah and Benjamin gathered at Jerusalem within the three days. It was the ninth month, on the twentieth day of the month. All the people sat in the open square before the house of God, trembling because of this matter and because of the heavy rain.
9:8.note: The winter downpour in the ninth month turns this gathering into a scene of physical misery, mirroring the emotional weight of their guilt.

9:9.rendering: Ezra stood and said to them: "You have acted unfaithfully and married foreign women, adding to Israel's guilt."
9:9.note: The Hebrew word for "unfaithfully" (ma'al) echoes the same term used for Israel's idolatry throughout the prophets, framing mixed marriages as a form of spiritual betrayal.

9:10.rendering: Now then, make confession to the LORD, the God of your fathers, and do his will. Separate yourselves from the peoples of the land and from the foreign women.
9:10.note: The Hebrew word for "confession" here literally means "give praise," turning public repentance into an act of honoring God.

9:11.rendering: Then all the assembly answered with a loud voice, "Yes, we must do just as you have said to us."
9:11.note: The Hebrew word for "assembly" echoes the great covenant gatherings at Sinai and in Deuteronomy, turning a moment of painful confession into a communal recommitment.

9:12.rendering: But the people are many, and it is a time of heavy rain. We cannot stand outside, and the task is not for one or two days, for we have greatly transgressed in this matter.
9:12.note: The people's practical objection reveals how even justified reform can feel overwhelming in the rain-soaked reality of ancient Judah.

9:13.rendering: Let our officials stand for the whole assembly. Let all who are in our cities and have taken foreign wives come at appointed times, together with the elders and judges of each city, until the fierce anger of our God over this matter is turned away from us.
9:13.note: This communal process of repentance echoes the covenant-renewal patterns of Deuteronomy, where leaders mediate for the people under threat of divine anger.

9:14.rendering: Only Jonathan the son of Asahel and Jahzeiah the son of Tikvah stood against this, and Meshullam and Shabbethai the Levite supported them.
9:14.note: The Hebrew word for "stood against" carries a sense of bold opposition, a rare pushback in a story of rapid communal reform.

9:15.rendering: So the returned exiles did this. Ezra the priest selected men, heads of their fathers' households, each of them identified by name. On the first day of the tenth month they sat down to investigate the matter.
9:15.note: The leaders' public, named accountability on a set day shows how communal repentance in Ezra's time demanded concrete structure, not just emotion.

9:16.rendering: They finished dealing with all the men who had married foreign women, from the first day of the tenth month until the first day of the first month.
9:16.note: This precise three-month window echoes the careful timelines of Israel's earlier reforms, showing how communal repentance requires both urgency and methodical patience.

9:17.rendering: Among the sons of the priests who had married foreign women were found these: of the sons of Jeshua son of Jozadak and his brothers: Maaseiah, Eliezer, Jarib, and Gedaliah.
9:17.note: This list opens with the high-priestly family, showing even the spiritual leaders had to confront the same compromise the whole community faced.

9:18.rendering: They gave their hands in pledge to send away their wives, and being guilty, they offered a ram from the flock as a guilt offering for their error.
9:18.note: The Hebrew literally says they "gave their hand" to divorce their foreign wives, an ancient gesture of solemn agreement that echoes covenant language throughout the Torah.

9:19.rendering: From the sons of Immer: Hanani and Zebadiah.
9:19.note: This terse list of guilty priests echoes the opening genealogy in Ezra 2, showing how even the returned holy line had become entangled in the very pollutions they escaped.

9:20.rendering: From the sons of Harim: Maaseiah, Elijah, Shemaiah, Jehiel, and Uzziah.
9:20.note: This list of guilty priests from Harim's family shows the reform cutting deepest where leadership was most compromised.

9:21.rendering: From the sons of Pashhur: Elioenai, Maaseiah, Ishmael, Nethanel, Jozabad, and Elasah.
9:21.note: This terse list of six names, pulled from a longer register of guilty priests, quietly echoes the weight of communal failure in the post-exile temple community.

9:22.rendering: Among the Levites: Jozabad, Shimei, Kelaiah (that is Kelita), Pethahiah, Judah, and Eliezer.
9:22.note: This list of Levites who divorced foreign wives echoes the earlier priestly names, showing how the reform reached even those tasked with teaching Israel the law.

9:23.rendering: From the singers: Eliashib. From the gatekeepers: Shallum, Telem, and Uri.
9:23.note: This short list of temple musicians and doorkeepers who divorced foreign wives quietly shows that even support roles in worship were not exempt from the community's painful reform.

9:24.rendering: From the sons of Parosh: Ramiah, Izziah, Malkijah, Mijamin, Eleazar, Malkijah, and Benaiah.
9:24.note: This list of names from a single clan shows the communal stakes of repentance, as entire households faced the hard choice together.

9:25.rendering: From the sons of Elam: Mattaniah, Zechariah, Jehiel, Abdi, Jeremoth, and Elijah.
9:25.note: This list of guilty names in Ezra 10 echoes the earlier census of returnees in chapter 2, turning communal restoration into painful, personal accountability.

9:26.rendering: Of the sons of Zattu: Elioenai, Eliashib, Mattaniah, Jeremoth, Zabad, and Aziza.
9:26.note: This list of names, drawn from an ancient Persian-era document, shows real families facing hard communal repentance after the exile.

9:27.rendering: From the sons of Bebai: Jehohanan, Hananiah, Zabbai, and Athlai.
9:27.note: This list of guilty men from Bebai's clan shows the communal cost of mixed marriages in post-exile Judah.

9:28.rendering: And of the sons of Pahath-moab: Adna, Kelal, Benaiah, Maaseiah, Mattaniah, Bezalel, Binnui, and Manasseh.
9:28.note: This list of names, each tied to a specific clan like Pahath-moab, underscores how communal repentance in Ezra touches entire households and lineages, not just lone individuals.

9:29.rendering: From the descendants of Pahath-Moab: Adna, Kelal, Benaiah, Maaseiah, Mattaniah, Bezalel, Binnui, and Manasseh.
9:29.note: This list of names, echoing the returnees in Ezra 2, shows the community owning their compromise after the exile.

9:30.rendering: From the sons of Harim: Eliezer, Ishijah, Malchijah, Shemaiah, Shimeon, Benjamin, Malluch, and Shemariah.
9:30.note: This list of guilty men from Harim's clan shows the painful communal cost of repentance after the exile.

9:31.rendering: From the sons of Harim: Eliezer, Ishijah, Malchijah, Shemaiah, Shimeon,
9:31.note: Harim's priestly line, tasked with temple service, now faces the painful cost of covenant renewal.

9:32.rendering: From the sons of Hashum: Mattenai, Mattattah, Zabad, Eliphelet, Jeremai, Manasseh, and Shimei.
9:32.note: This list of names appears in a corporate confession where the community owns its collective failure to keep the covenant pure.

9:33.rendering: From the sons of Bani: Maadai, Amram, Uel, Benaiah, Bedeiah, Cheluhi, Vaniah, Meremoth, Eliashib, Mattaniah, Mattenai, Jaasu, Bani, Binnui, Shimei, Shelemiah, Nathan, Adaiah, Machnadebai, Shashai, Sharai, Azarel, Shelemiah, Shemariah, Shallum, Amariah, and Joseph.
9:33.note: Ezra lists these twenty-seven guilty men from the clan of Bani with the same care the earlier chapters used to name returning exiles, showing that covenant renewal requires naming sin as precisely as God once named his restored people.

9:34.note: Ezra 10 lists these names in the grim post-exile reckoning of Israelite men who divorced their foreign wives to renew covenant purity.

9:35.note: This list of guilty men in the post-exile community reads like a roll call of shame, showing how even the returned leaders had to face public covenant failure.

9:36.rendering: And of the sons of Bani: Mattenai, Amram, Uel,
9:36.note: This list of names marks the painful communal choice to dissolve mixed marriages, echoing the covenant renewal at Sinai.

9:37.rendering: And among the sons of Bani: Maadai, Amram, Uel, Benaiah, Bedeiah, Keluhi, Vaniah, Meremoth, Eliashib, Mattaniah, Mattenai, and Jaasu, and Bani and Binnui, Shimei, Shelemiah, Nathan, Adaiah, Machnadebai, Shashai, Sharai, Azarel, Shelemiah, Shemariah, Shallum, Amariah, and Joseph.
9:37.note: Ezra lists these men from Bani's clan among those who had married foreign wives, showing how the community's leaders addressed the crisis one household at a time.

9:38.note: Ezra 10 lists the guilty as family heads, showing how covenant failure rippled through entire households in post-exile Judah.

9:39.note: This list of names in Ezra 10 functions like a public ledger of repentance, showing how even the smallest households participated in the community's costly return to covenant faithfulness.

9:40.note: These names, buried in a long list of those who sent away foreign wives, quietly echo the theme of costly communal repentance after exile.

9:41.rendering: Meshullam, Malluk, Adaiah, Jashub, Sheal, and Jeremoth.
9:41.note: This list of guilty men closes with a final group of six names, echoing the careful record-keeping of ancient covenant communities.

9:42.rendering: Of the sons of Nebo: Jeiel, Mattithiah, Zabad, Zebina, Jaddai, Joel, and Benaiah.
9:42.note: This list of returned exiles from Nebo shows the painful communal cost of restoring covenant faithfulness after the Babylonian exile.

9:43.rendering: All these men had taken foreign wives, and some of them had wives by whom they had children.
9:43.note: The terse ending leaves the painful consequences of the mass divorces hanging, inviting readers to wrestle with the cost of covenant renewal.
